title-group.article-title: Reassessing home blood pressure thresholds: clinical implications of lowering the diagnostic criteria to 130/80 mmHg

article-meta.aff: aDivision of Cardiology, Department of Internal Medicine, Dongguk University Ilsan Hospital, Dongguk University, Goyang

bTransdisciplinary Major in Learning Health Systems, Department of Public Health Science, Graduate School, and Department of Health Policy and Management, College of Health Science, Korea University Seoul, Korea

author-notes.corresp: Correspondence to Moo-Yong Rhee, MD, PhD, Division of Cardiology, Department of Internal Medicine, Dongguk University Ilsan Hospital, 27 Dongguk-ro, Ilsandong-gu, Goyang 10326, Republic of Korea. Tel: +82 31 961 7125; fax: +82 31 961 7785; e-mail: mooyong.rhee@gmail.com

permissions.copyright-statement: Copyright © 2026 The Author(s). Published by Wolters Kluwer Health, Inc.

license.license-p: This is an open access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution-Non Commercial-No Derivatives License 4.0 (CCBY-NC-ND), where it is permissible to download and share the work provided it is properly cited. The work cannot be changed in any way or used commercially without permission from the journal. http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0

sec.title: Objectives:
sec.p: This study investigated whether lowering the home blood pressure (BP) threshold for the diagnosis of hypertension from 135/85 to 130/80 mmHg enhances diagnostic accuracy when assessed against ambulatory BP monitoring (ABPM).

sec.title: Methods:
sec.p: A total of 646 untreated participants (mean age 52 ± 10 years; 310 men) with valid 3-day office BP, 7-day home BP, and 24-h ABPM data and preserved renal function were included. Hypertension phenotypes were classified as normotension, white-coat, masked, and sustained hypertension according to office BP and ABPM criteria.

sec.title: Results:
sec.p: Lowering the home BP threshold increased sensitivity from 72.3 to 89.5% but reduced specificity from 81.8 to 69.1%, thereby improving overall diagnostic accuracy from 73.1 to 87.8% and the kappa coefficient from 0.238 to 0.427. At the conventional threshold of 135/85 mmHg, 63.2% of masked and 15.1% of sustained hypertension were misclassified as normotension, whereas these rates declined to 30.3 and 3.4%, respectively, at the 130/80 mmHg threshold. Individuals with home BP between 130/80 and 134/84 mmHg showed intermediate office and ambulatory BP values, with a high prevalence of masked (32.9%) and sustained hypertension (11.7%). Within this subgroup, isolated nighttime and daytime–nighttime hypertension were identified in 35.7 and 13.5% of participants, respectively.

sec.title: Conclusion:
sec.p: The conventional home BP threshold of 135/85 mmHg may fail to identify a considerable proportion of masked, sustained, and nighttime hypertension. Lowering the threshold to 130/80 mmHg, or designating 130/80–134/84 mmHg as a diagnostic ‘gray zone’ warranting ABPM confirmation, may improve diagnostic precision and facilitate earlier detection of hypertension in clinical practice.

sec.title: INTRODUCTION
sec.p: Hypertension affects 1.3 billion adults globally and remains the leading modifiable risk factor for cardiovascular diseases, accounting for 10.8 million deaths annually [1]. Despite advances in blood pressure (BP) measurement technology, diagnostic misclassification remains a critical barrier to optimal cardiovascular prevention. Office BP measurement, although convenient, fails to detect masked hypertension, a phenotype affecting 10–30% of adults with normal clinical readings [2–4].

Masked hypertension is independently associated with an increased risk of hypertension-mediated organ damage and confers an approximately two-fold higher cardiovascular event risk than true normotension [5]. The clinical significance lies in the frequent underdiagnosis of patients with masked hypertension in routine practice, who remain untreated despite their elevated cardiovascular risk profile. Therefore, contemporary hypertension guidelines advocate out-of-office BP assessments, including home BP monitoring (HBPM) and ambulatory BP monitoring (ABPM), to identify occult hypertension in patients with high-normal office readings or established masked hypertension risk factors [6–10].

Out-of-office BP monitoring has emerged as an essential tool for the comprehensive diagnosis of hypertension. Although 24-h ABPM provides the diagnostic gold standard, practical constraints limit its widespread implementation. HBPM offers a feasible alternative, demonstrating superior reproducibility and stronger cardiovascular risk prediction compared with office BP monitoring [11,12]. The clinical utility of HBPM is well established, yet its integration into routine practice varies considerably across healthcare systems worldwide. Japan exemplifies successful, systematic implementation, where HBPM has been incorporated into both clinical care and public health strategies, with over 35 million devices distributed nationwide [13]. By contrast, data from the United States National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (NHANES, 2011–2014) revealed that only 24.7% of the US population utilized HBPM, with significant socioeconomic disparities: adults with an income-to-poverty ratio below 1.30 demonstrated markedly lower rates of HBPM use compared to those in higher income brackets [14]. In many low-resource settings, HBPM remains largely unavailable in primary care, further limiting its potential for hypertension diagnosis and management. These disparities – between successful national implementation and persistent access barriers – underscore the critical need to expand equitable access to HBPM, which is essential for improving early hypertension detection and strengthening population-wide blood pressure control. Despite the convenience and superior prognostic value of HBPM compared with office BP measurements, HBPM often yields discordant diagnostic results compared with ABPM [15].

In an outcome-based study, the diagnostic threshold for HBPM for hypertension was identified as 131.9/82.4 mmHg [16]. This is approximately 3 mmHg lower in SBP and 2.5 mmHg lower in DBP than the current HBPM diagnostic threshold of 135/85 mmHg, suggesting that individuals with BP levels in this intermediate range may be misclassified as normotensive if using the conventional HBPM criteria.

Recently, a hypertension practice guideline proposed a revised diagnostic threshold for HBPM of 130/80 mmHg while maintaining an office BP threshold of 140/90 mmHg [17]. Previous study has shown that lowering the HBPM threshold to 130/80 mmHg may reduce the prevalence of masked home hypertension, in which home BP appears normal, but ABPM indicates hypertension [18]. Moreover, patients with discrepant HBPM and ABPM results have elevated office BP, indicating a high cardiovascular risk [15].

Therefore, adopting a lower diagnostic threshold for HBPM may improve the detection of patients with ambulatory hypertension who would otherwise be misdiagnosed as home-normotensive. Identifying these individuals would facilitate earlier interventions, including lifestyle modifications, to reduce their BP; otherwise, those are left untreated because of a false diagnosis of home normotension. This study aimed to determine whether lowering the diagnostic threshold for HBPM improves the diagnosis of hypertension in treatment-naive adults with suspected hypertension.

sec.title: Study population
sec.p: Data from the two studies were combined for analysis. The first study was conducted between March 2012 and September 2013 at four clinical trial centers in Korea (Dongguk University Ilsan Hospital, Inje University Ilsan Paik Hospital, Myongji Hospital, and Seoul Medical Center) [18]. The second study was conducted between January 2015 and December 2019 at a single center (Dongguk University Ilsan Hospital) and was registered at ClinicalTrials. gov (NCT03855605) [19].

The inclusion and exclusion criteria were the same for both studies. Individuals who were suspected of having hypertension (office BP measured by physicians at the outpatient clinic ≥140/90 mmHg), were not being managed on antihypertensive drugs, and were aged at least 20 years were prospectively enrolled in those studies. Individuals with secondary hypertension, hypertensive emergency or urgency, heart failure (New York Heart Association class III and IV), clinically significant cardiac arrhythmia, impaired renal function (serum creatinine ≥1.7 mg/dl), pregnancy, participating in night labor or shift work, history of drug or alcohol abuse within 6 months, current participation in other clinical studies, taking other clinical trial drugs within the previous month, and taking medications known to affect BP, such as steroids, monoamine oxidase inhibitors, oral contraceptives, or sympathomimetics, were excluded. The institutional review boards of the participating hospitals reviewed and approved the study protocols and obtained informed consent. All the participants provided written informed consent before participating in the study.

sec.title: Measurements for office, home, and ambulatory blood pressure
sec.p: The BP measurement protocol was previously outlined, and no significant procedural differences were observed between the studies. The measurement schedule is illustrated in Supplementary Figure 1. Trained nurses conducted office BP measurements during the first, second (following the completion of home BP monitoring), and third visits (following 24-h ambulatory BP monitoring). Validated oscillometric devices were used, specifically the WatchBP Home (Microlife, Taiwan) in the first study and WatchBP Office (Microlife) in the second [20,21]. Participants were advised to abstain from smoking, avoid consuming caffeine-containing beverages, and refrain from engaging in physical activity for at least 30 min before the measurements. After a 5-min seated rest in a quiet room, three office BP readings were obtained at each visit, with a 1-min interval between measurements.

In the first study, BP was measured in both arms at the first visit, and the arm with the higher BP was designated as the index arm. At the subsequent visits, office BP was obtained from the index arm, and these measurements were used to calculate the office BP averages. At each visit, three BP readings were taken and averaged, and the office BP used for analysis was the mean of the three visit averages. In the second study, BP was measured simultaneously in both arms at all three visits, with three readings obtained per arm at each visit. At each visit, three BP readings obtained from each arm were averaged separately. For each arm, the three visit averages were then calculated, and the arm with the higher overall mean BP was designated as the index arm. BP from the index arm was used for office BP analysis. Both studies monitored Home BP using a validated oscillometric device (Watch BP Home; Microlife). Participants were instructed to take triplicate readings at 1-min intervals every morning (between 07 : 00 or upon waking and 09 : 00) and evening (between 21 : 00 and 23 : 00 or before bedtime) for 7 days. Morning BP measurements were obtained after micturition or defecation and before showering and breakfast. On the final day of home BP monitoring, the participants measured their last morning home BP and attended the clinical trial center for the second visit. Based on our previous findings, a valid home BP measurement was defined as at least 5 days of morning and evening measurements. The morning and evening home BP readings were averaged. Although current hypertension guidelines generally recommend performing home BP measurements on the left arm [22], participants in both studies were instructed to measure home BP on the arm with the higher office BP at the first visit. This approach was chosen to maintain consistency between home BP and office BP measurements performed on the same arm and to improve diagnostic accuracy.

ABPM was conducted for 25 h on the nondominant arm in accordance with guideline recommendations [22], at the second visit using an automated, noninvasive oscillometric device (Mobil-O-Graph, I.E.M GmbH, Germany), with measurements obtained at 30-min intervals [23]. Participants were advised to continue their usual daily activities. A valid ABPM record required greater than 70% of the total measurement attempts, including at least 14 readings during the daytime (10 : 00–20 : 00 for the first study and 09 : 00–21 : 00 for the second study) and a minimum of seven readings at night (00 : 00–06 : 00). Blood samples for hematological and biochemical analyses were collected after at least 8 h of overnight fasting.

sec.title: Definition of hypertension phenotypes
sec.p: Hypertension by office BP was defined as an average SBP at least 140 mmHg and/or DBP at least 90 mmHg based on the mean BP values from the three office visits. Ambulatory hypertension was defined as 24-h averaged SBP at least 130 mmHg and/or DBP at least 80 mmHg, and/or daytime averaged SBP at least 135 mmHg and/or DBP at least 85 mmHg, and or nighttime averaged SBP at least 120 mmHg and/or at least 70 mmHg. Ambulatory hypertension was used as the reference standard for the diagnosis of hypertension. The definitions of hypertension phenotypes, normotension, white-coat hypertension, masked hypertension, and sustained hypertension based on office and ambulatory BP are defined in Supplementary Table 1. Ambulatory normotension (normotension), isolated daytime hypertension (IDHT), isolated nighttime hypertension (INHT), and day-nighttime hypertension (DNHT) are defined in Supplementary Table 2.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: The following three definitions as diagnostic thresholds of home hypertension were evaluated; averaged home SBP at least 135 mmHg and/or averaged home DBP at least 85 mm Hg, averaged home SBP at least 130 mmHg and/or averaged home DBP at least 85 mmHg, and averaged home SBP at least 130 mmHg and/or averaged home DBP at least 80 mmHg. The sensitivity, specificity, and positive-predictive and negative-predictive values of hypertension according to each diagnostic threshold of home BP measurement were calculated using the diagnosis of hypertension based on ambulatory BP (averaged 24-h and/or daytime and/or nighttime BP) as the reference standard. The degree of diagnostic agreement between home and ambulatory BP was determined by calculating diagnostic accuracy [percentage of (true positive + true negative)/all] and using Cohen's kappa (κ) coefficient. Differences among the different diagnostic thresholds were analyzed using the Chi-squared test with Bonferroni adjustment or the McNemar test, as appropriate. The kappa (κ) coefficients were compared under the assumption of z-distribution. The study population was divided into three groups based on home BP; less than 130/80, 130/80–134/84, and at least 135/85 mmHg. Clinical characteristics and demographic features were compared using ANOVA and chi-squared tests. All statistical analyses were performed using SAS (version 9.4; SAS Institute, Cary, North Carolina, USA) and MedCalc (version 23.1.7 (MedCalc Software BVBA, Ostend, Belgium).

sec.title: RESULTS
sec.p: The diagnostic sensitivity increased with a threshold of 130/80 mmHg, whereas the specificity showed a decreasing trend. The 130/80 mmHg threshold demonstrated the highest sensitivity [89.5%, 95% confidence interval (CI) 86.8–91.9%] and accuracy (87.8%) with moderate agreement (κ = 0.427). Contrastingly, the 135/85 mmHg threshold exhibited the highest specificity (81.8%, 95% CI 69.1–90.9%) but the lowest sensitivity (72.3%, 95% CI 68.5–75.8) and agreement (κ = 0.238). The positive predictive value remained consistently high across all thresholds (>96.9%), whereas the negative-predictive value varied with the highest value (38%) at the 130/80 mmHg threshold (Table 1).

caption.p: Sensitivity, specificity, positive-predictive value, and negative-predictive value of various home blood pressure thresholds, using ambulatory blood pressure as the reference standard

fn.p: Different superscripts, a and b, represent statistically significant differences (P < 0.05) based on the Bonferroni method. The chi-square test obtained P values of sensitivity, specificity, PPP, and NPV; the P value of Kappa was tested under the assumption of z distribution. CI, confidence interval; PPV, positive-predictive value; NPV, negative-predictive value.

sec.p: The distribution of the hypertension phenotypes changed with different home BP thresholds (Table 2). When using the 135/85 mmHg threshold, 63.2% of masked hypertension and 15.1% of sustained hypertension cases were misclassified as home normotension cases. Conversely, these proportions were significantly reduced at the 130/80 mmHg threshold, with only 30.3% of masked hypertension cases and 3.4% of sustained hypertension cases categorized as home normotensive. The distribution of the hypertension phenotypes, ambulatory normotension, IDHT, INHT, and DNHT showed a similar tendency. With a 135/85 mmHg threshold, home normotension included 65.6% IDHT, 75.7% INHT, and 18.4% DNHT. These proportions notably decreased to 31.3% for IDHT, 40% for INHT, and 4.9% for DNHT using the 130/80 mmHg threshold.

caption.p: Distribution of hypertension phenotypes according to different diagnostic thresholds of home blood pressure

tr.td: Based on office and ambulatory blood pressure

fn.p: P values tested by the McNemar test.

sec.p: Participants’ demographic and clinical characteristics according to these home BP levels (<130/80, 130/80–134/84, and ≥135/85 mmHg) are summarized in Table 3. Age varied significantly across groups (P < 0.001), with the at least 135/85 mmHg group being younger (mean age; 50.6 ± 9.8 years) compared to the less than 130/80 mmHg (55.5 ± 10.6 years) and 130/80–134/84 mmHg (54.3 ± 9.1 years) groups. The proportion of men was significantly higher in the 130/80–134/84 mmHg group compared to the less than 130/80 mmHg group, but similar to the at least 135/85 mmHg group (20 vs. 44 and 55.4%, respectively; P < 0.001). No significant differences were observed in the prevalence of diabetes mellitus, estimated glomerular filtration rate, or fasting blood glucose levels. Notably, office BP, 24-h ambulatory BP, and daytime/nighttime BP consistently increased across the three home BP categories (all P < 0.001). Specifically, office SBP/DBP progressively increased from 131.8 ± 8.6/81.7 ± 6.2 mmHg in the <130/80 mmHg group to 136.7 ± 8.0/87.2 ± 6 mmHg in the 130/80–134/84 mmHg group and 145.2 ± 10.6/95.3 ± 8.0 mmHg in the at least 135/85 mmHg group.

caption.p: Clinical characteristics stratified by home blood pressure levels

fn.p: The different superscript letters (a, b, and c) represent significant differences between groups by the posthoc pairwise comparison. eGFR, estimated glomerular filtration rate; FBG, fasting blood glucose.
P by ANOVA or chi-square test.

sec.p: Among individuals in the 130/80–134/84 mmHg home BP group, greater than 93% exhibited ambulatory hypertension, with 46.8% diagnosed with masked hypertension and 46.8% with sustained hypertension. Contrastingly, among individuals with a home BP less than 130/80 mmHg, the prevalence of masked hypertension was 47%, whereas the prevalence of sustained hypertension was substantially lower at 15% (Fig. 1). In the home BP 130/80–134/84 mmHg group, 22.9% had INHT and 10.1% had IDHT. The prevalence of DNHT was substantially higher in the 130/80–134/84 mmHg group (60.6%) than in the less than 130/80 mmHg group (24%) (Fig. 2).

caption.p: Distribution of hypertension phenotypes across different home blood pressure levels. Hypertension phenotypes are classified based on office and ambulatory blood pressure measurements. NT, normotension (office normotension and ambulatory normotension); WH, white-coat hypertension (office hypertension and ambulatory normotension); MH, masked hypertension (office normotension and ambulatory hypertension); SH, sustained hypertension (office and ambulatory hypertension).

Distribution of hypertension phenotypes across different home blood pressure levels. Hypertension phenotypes are classified based on ambulatory blood pressure measurements. NT, ambulatory normotension (daytime and nighttime normotension); IDHT, isolated daytime hypertension (daytime hypertension but nighttime normotension); INHT, isolated nighttime hypertension (daytime normotension and nighttime hypertension); DNHT, combined daytime–nighttime hypertension (daytime and nighttime hypertension).

sec.p: Hypertension phenotype distributions across home BP categories are shown in Table 4. Normotension was predominantly found in individuals with home BP less than 130/80 mmHg (82.5%), while sustained hypertension was most frequent in the at least 135/85 mmHg group (84.9%), whereas masked hypertension was relatively evenly distributed across categories. Ambulatory normotension was primarily observed in the less than 130/80 mmHg group (69.1%), whereas DNHT dominated in the at least 135/85 mmHg group (81.6%). INHT showed a relatively even distribution. In the intermediate home BP levels (130/80–134/84 mmHg), more than 30% of individuals had masked hypertension or INHT. Moreover, the prevalence of sustained hypertension or DNHT was more than triple and double compared to the home BP level less than 130 mmHg.

caption.p: Distribution of hypertension phenotypes across three home blood pressure categories

tr.td: Office and ambulatory blood pressure classification

sec.title: Mechanistic insights and clinical implications
sec.p: This study demonstrated that lowering the diagnostic threshold for home BP measurements from 135/85–130/80 mmHg significantly enhanced the detection of ambulatory hypertension without a significant loss of specificity. The 130/80 mmHg threshold yielded a sensitivity of 89.5% (95% CI 86.8–91.9%), compared with 72.3% (95% CI 68.5–75.8%) for the conventional threshold; a 24% relative improvement that could identify an additional 75 000 individuals with hypertension per million screened. Importantly, this increased sensitivity was accompanied by a high positive-predictive value of 97%, supporting the clinical reliability of initiating interventions based on home BP readings at least 130/80 mmHg.

A particularly striking observation concerns individuals with home BP readings in the intermediate range of 130–134/80–84 mmHg. In this group, 93.6% of the patients had ambulatory hypertension, a prevalence comparable to that observed at higher home BP levels. These findings suggest that classifying such individuals as normotensive based solely on home BP measurements may result in substantial underdiagnosis and undertreatment. Considering the high probability of ambulatory hypertension, individuals within this range of home BP should undergo confirmatory ABPM if available, or should be considered for direct hypertension diagnosis and initiation of lifestyle modifications in resource-constrained settings.

This intermediate range was selected for analysis in our study because the International Database of Home Blood Pressure in relation to Cardiovascular Outcome (IDHOCO) identified an outcome-driven home BP threshold for hypertension near 130/80 mmHg (131.9/82.4 mmHg) [16], and previous reports of Stergiou and colleagues have characterized the 130–135/80–85 mmHg range as a borderline zone for home BP [24,25]. Furthermore, our prior research demonstrated that applying a 130/80 mmHg home BP threshold substantially reduced the prevalence of masked home hypertension. Together, these findings justified defining 130–134/80–84 mmHg as a clinically meaningful gray zone for further evaluation in the present study.

Although HBPM has improved the detection of masked hypertension and eliminated the white-coat effect, it has inherent limitations. HBPM captures discrete daytime measurements and may miss important BP phenotypes, including diurnal variability, exercise-induced elevations, and nocturnal hypertension. Notably, 40% of the individuals with a home BP less than 130/80 mmHg in our cohort demonstrated isolated nocturnal hypertension, a phenotype associated with a 38% increased cardiovascular risk that remains undetectable through daytime home measurements alone [26]. This finding emphasizes that HBPM serves a complementary rather than a substitutive role to ABPM in comprehensive hypertension.

sec.title: Alignment with contemporary guideline and evidence
sec.p: Current hypertension guidelines show discrepancies in the recommended thresholds for diagnosing hypertension using HBPM. The 2019 Japanese Society of Hypertension Guidelines established a diagnostic threshold of at least 135/85 mmHg for home BP [9]. European guidelines retained the threshold of 135/85 mmHg for home BP diagnosis [7,8], whereas the 2017 American college of cardiology (ACC)/American heart association (AHA) guidelines recommended considering this threshold as equivalent to stage 2 hypertension (i.e. ≥140/90 mmHg) by lowering the office BP threshold of stage 1 hypertension to at least 130/80 mmHg [10]. The 2020 Brazilian guidelines explicitly adopted 130/80 mmHg as the threshold for a home BP diagnosis [17]. This study proposes such a progressive approach, demonstrating that an HBPM diagnostic threshold of at least 130/80 mmHg correlates significantly better with ambulatory hypertension than traditional criteria.

Currently, the diagnostic threshold of home BP was determined by identifying BP levels corresponding to the same cardiovascular risk as office BP of at least 140/90 mmHg [27]. However, whether the diagnostic threshold for hypertension based on home or ambulatory BP must be aligned with BP in the clinic should be reviewed. Out-of-office BP measurements can identify masked and white-coat hypertension, conditions that are not adequately captured by office BP alone. Therefore, conventional office BP-based thresholds inherently include patients with white-coat hypertension but exclude those with masked hypertension, potentially skewing the outcomes. Conversely, home BP and ambulatory BP measurements exclude white-coat hypertension but identify masked hypertension. Therefore, although the determination was based on the risk of cardiovascular disease, whether it is appropriate to estimate the home and ambulatory BP corresponding to the office BP of at least 140/90 mmHg must be considered.

Although hypertension guidelines have traditionally maintained a home BP diagnostic threshold of 135/85 mmHg, the representative outcome-based study, the IDHOCO, suggested an optimal threshold of 131.8/82.4 mmHg based on cardiovascular outcomes [16]. Consequently, individuals whose BP is between the threshold from the IDHOCO study and the current diagnostic threshold standard of 135/85 mmHg, who require active lifestyle modifications to lower BP, might otherwise be diagnosed as normotensive and thus remain untreated. Therefore, individuals whose BP is between the IDHOCO-calculated BP and current diagnostic threshold of 135/85 mmHg are likely to be diagnosed with normal BP and remain untreated even with lifestyle modifications to lower BP.

sec.title: Public health implications
sec.p: Optimizing the diagnosis of hypertension using home BP measurement has significant public health implications. Hypertension is the leading modifiable risk factor for stroke and ischemic heart disease worldwide [28,29]. Out-of-office measurements facilitate proper identification and management of masked and white-coat hypertension. HBPM offers convenience and lower costs than ABPM. Japan provides a representative example of the feasibility of implementing HBPM at the population level; by 2001, more than 35 million home BP monitors had already been distributed, nearly equivalent to Japan's hypertensive population [13]. Recent surveys in Japan have demonstrated high penetration rates, with home BP monitors owned by 77% of hypertensive and 41% of normotensive individuals [30]. However, as demonstrated by the present and previous studies, the differences between home and ambulatory BP measurements must be considered in diagnosing hypertension based on home measurements. Although there may be disagreements over whether diagnosing hypertension using ABPM is better than using HBPM [12,31], ABPM can define nocturnal hypertension, which strongly correlates with the risk of cardiovascular disease [32]. Hence, ABPM is appropriate for the diagnosis of hypertension. However, HBPM, particularly when combined with patient education, demonstrated superior diagnostic accuracy compared with office BP measurement, showing higher sensitivity (90 vs. 81%) and specificity (84 vs. 76%) when ABPM was used as the reference standard [33].

Although lowering the diagnostic threshold of home BP to 130/80 mmHg may increase false-positive rates and raise concerns regarding potential overtreatment, comprehensive risk–benefit analyses consistently favor this approach. Using the 2017 ACC/AHA hypertension guidelines and treatment recommendations, analysis of US national health and nutrition examination survey data revealed a 13.7% increase in hypertension diagnoses; however, only a 1.9% increase in the proportion recommended for antihypertensive treatment [34]. Consequently, appropriately recommending lifestyle modifications or subsequent ABPM for patients within the home BP range of 130–135/80–85 mmHg may reduce the risk of overtreatment.

Furthermore, this shift in diagnostic thresholds is strongly supported by economic evidence, as early management costs are considerably lower than treatment costs for advanced cardiovascular diseases resulting from undiagnosed hypertension. Economic modeling studies across various healthcare systems have indicated substantial net cost savings, improved cardiovascular outcomes, and quality-adjusted life-year gains from early detection and prompt intervention [35,36]. Clinically and from a population health perspective, lowering the home BP diagnostic threshold to 130/80 mmHg may address cardiovascular risks and provide long-term health and economic benefits.

Additionally, although HBPM is widely used among patients in Korea, it is not reimbursed by the national health insurance system, which limits its widespread adoption in routine clinical practice. In contrast, ambulatory BP monitoring is reimbursed but remains limited in use because of cost and inconvenience. Nevertheless, HBPM offers substantial advantages by reducing overtreatment due to white-coat hypertension and enabling early detection of masked hypertension, thereby lowering long-term cardiovascular risk and healthcare expenditures. An insurance-supported HBPM rental program implemented in France demonstrated the feasibility of a widely adoptable model to increase the use of home BP devices for the diagnosis and management of hypertension in clinical practice [37]. For low-income and middle-income countries, such approaches may offer a practical model to strengthen hypertension detection and management. However, because reimbursement for HBPM remains limited in many health systems, the real-world applicability of lower HBPM diagnostic thresholds is likely to vary substantially depending on resource availability.

sec.title: Study strengths and limitations
sec.p: This study has several methodological strengths, including its prospective design, standardized measurement protocols, and the use of ABPM, which measures nocturnal BP, as the reference standard for hypertension diagnosis. However, this study had several limitations. First, the study included treatment-naive patients referred for hypertension evaluation, potentially limiting the generalizability to the broader community or treated patients. Second, although ABPM-confirmed hypertension strongly predicts cardiovascular events, this study did not assess clinical outcomes. Prospective outcome data across varying home BP thresholds are needed to definitively establish the optimal diagnostic threshold. Third, although ABPM represents the best available reference standard, it has inherent limitations, including measurement variability, poor intra-individual reproducibility, potential for technical artifacts, and inability to capture long-term BP patterns. Fourth, a minor procedural difference in office BP measurement existed between the two studies. In the first study, BP was measured only in the index arm, which had been determined at the initial visit, and all subsequent office BP measurements were obtained from this arm. In contrast, the second study measured BP in both arms at each visit, and the readings from the three visits were averaged separately for each arm; the arm with the higher averaged BP was then used for office BP classification. Although such differences could theoretically introduce slight variability, further analysis of the second study showed that only 10 of 391 participants (2.6%) changed their office BP–based hypertension classification when using the index arm defined at the first visit, all shifting from sustained to masked hypertension. Given the very small magnitude of this change and the consistent standardization of HBPM and ABPM protocols across both studies, these procedural differences are unlikely to have affected the main findings. Fifth, the single-country recruitment may affect generalizability, although the physiological relationship between home and ambulatory BP remains consistent across populations. Finally, although home BP measurement methods were protocol-driven and standardized in this study, adherence to such protocols in routine clinical practice may vary significantly, affecting diagnostic accuracy.

sec.title: Future directions
sec.p: Our findings highlighted several critical priorities for future research. Large-scale longitudinal studies assessing cardiovascular outcomes across varying home BP thresholds are essential for evidence-based guideline revision. Such studies should specifically evaluate whether the improved diagnostic sensitivity of the 130/80 mmHg threshold translates into reduced cardiovascular events and mortality. Population-specific analyses are required to determine whether tailored diagnostic thresholds are appropriate for high-risk subgroups, including elderly individuals and patients with diabetes mellitus, chronic kidney disease, or established cardiovascular disease. These populations may benefit from even lower diagnostic thresholds given their elevated baseline risk. Health economic evaluations in diverse healthcare settings are critical for guiding policy implementation, particularly in resource-limited environments, in which ABPM availability is restricted. Cost-effectiveness analyses should consider direct medical costs and the broader societal impacts of improved hypertension detection and control.

In conclusion, lowering the diagnostic threshold of home hypertension from 135/85–130/80 mmHg significantly enhances the diagnostic sensitivity and accurately identifies individuals who otherwise remain untreated despite an elevated cardiovascular risk. We suggest lowering the diagnostic threshold of home BP to 130/80 mmHg or designating the home BP range between 130/80 and 134/84 mmHg as a diagnostic ‘gray zone’ requiring ABPM confirmation.

sec.title: Conflicts of interest
sec.p: There are no conflicts of interest.

fn.p: Abbreviations: ABPM, ambulatory blood pressure monitoring; ACC, American College of Cardiology; AHA, American Heart Association; BP, blood pressure; DNHT, day-nighttime hypertension; HBPM, home blood pressure monitoring; IDHOCO, International Database of Home Blood Pressure in Relation to Cardiovascular Outcome; IDHT, isolated daytime hypertension; INHT, isolated nighttime hypertension; WH, white-coat hypertension
Supplemental digital content is available for this article.

ref.mixed-citation: LiuFLuX. Hypertension and human health: evidence and prospects. Chronic Dis Transl Med 2024; 10:89–91.38872759 10.1002/cdt3.129PMC11166676
